content.problemStatement:
You are given a **binary** string SSS of length NNN and an integer KKK. You can perform the following operation on the string:


Select a prefix of the string SSS and flip all the characters of the prefix. A flip corresponds to changing 000 to 111 and vice-versa.
Find the **minimum** number of operations required to obtain a substring of length KKK such that all characters of the substring are 111.

Note:


A prefix is obtained by deleting some (possibly zero) characters from the end of the string.
A substring is obtained by deleting some (possibly zero) characters from the beginning of the string and some (possibly zero) characters from the end of the string.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK — the length of the string and the length length of substring containing all 111s we want to achieve.
The next line contains the binary string SSS.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the **minimum** number of operations required to obtain a substring of length KKK such that all characters of the substring are 111.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 3⋅1053\cdot 10^53⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: We need to obtain a substring containing three 111s. Using one operation, we can select the prefix S[1,3]S[1,3]S[1,3] and flip all the characters in the prefix. Thus, the string becomes 111111111.
Test case 222: The string already contains a substring having two 111s. Thus, we do not need any operation.
Test case 333: We need to obtain a substring containing three 111s. We can do so using 222 operations:

Operation 111: Select the prefix S[1,4]S[1, 4]S[1,4] and flip all characters in the prefix. Thus, the string becomes 010110101101011.
Operation 222: Select the prefix S[1,3]S[1, 3]S[1,3] and flip all characters in the prefix. Thus, the string becomes 101111011110111.

Thus, we have a substring S[3,5]S[3,5]S[3,5] of length 333 where all characters of the substring are 111. It can be shown that we cannot obtain such substring using less than 222 operations.
